claim: The U.S. 250th anniversary is less than five months away (July 4, 2026).
explanation: Date math aligns with the speech's timing and the 1776 to 2026 anniversary framing.

claim: Inflation was at "record levels" when he took office.
explanation: FactCheck.org reports inflation was about 3% when he took office and notes historic peaks were far higher (e.g., 1920).

claim: "Zero illegal aliens admitted" in the past nine months.
explanation: CBP reported zero Border Patrol releases for a period, but that does not mean zero illegal entries or zero releases by other pathways (and does not measure "got-aways").

claim: Fentanyl "flow" across the border is down 56% in one year.
explanation: Total illicit flow is not directly measurable; seizure data changed, but it cannot prove the stated percent change in total flow.

claim: 2025 murder rate had the biggest one-year decline; "lowest in 125 years."
explanation: Largest decline is likely, but "lowest in 125 years" is less certain (data comparability + final FBI 2025 report timing).

claim: Biden/Democrats produced the "worst inflation in history."
explanation: Worst U.S. inflation was far higher historically; inflation had cooled to around 3% by Trump's start.

claim: Core inflation is the lowest in more than five years.
explanation: CBS reports core inflation around 2.5% and describes it as the lowest since March 2021 (about five years).

claim: Core inflation was 1.7% in the last three months of 2025.
explanation: CBS reports sourcing is unclear and available CPI/core CPI figures do not show that number as stated.

claim: Gas is below $2.30 in most states; some $1.99; saw $1.85 in Iowa.
explanation: AAA statewide averages did not show most states below $2.30; Iowa average was higher; GasBuddy found only a handful of stations under $2 and the Iowa station claim was contradicted.

claim: Mortgage rates are the lowest in four years.
explanation: Freddie Mac PMMS shows about 6.01% (Feb 19, 2026), while in March 2022 rates were about 3.85% (within four years and lower).

claim: Typical new mortgage annual cost is down "almost $5,000" since he took office.
explanation: Requires defining "typical" (loan size, down payment, term, baseline date). The specific $5,000 figure is not pinned down in the cited fact checks.

claim: Secured more than $18 trillion in investment commitments in 12 months.
explanation: CBS found no evidence near $18T; a White House list totals $9.6T and includes questionable inclusions (some predate his term).

claim: Biden got less than $1T in new foreign direct investment over four years.
explanation: CBS cites BEA data indicating Biden-era FDI inflows add up to under $1T (as framed).

claim: Oil production is up by more than 600,000 barrels/day.
explanation: FactCheck.org cites EIA data showing an increase closer to about 334,600 bpd over the compared period.

claim: Natural gas production is at an all-time high because of his "drill" policy.
explanation: Production is very high, but it was already at record levels before he took office; trends were in motion.

claim: More Americans are working than ever; all new jobs are private-sector.
explanation: Record employed is true in raw count, but population growth matters; labor force participation is roughly flat and unemployment slightly higher.

claim: He "lifted" 2.4 million people off food stamps.
explanation: The number matches a projection of people losing eligibility due to work requirements, not people lifted by prosperity.

claim: Minnesota: "Somali community" pillaged $19B from taxpayers.
explanation: Figure refers to total federal funding for many programs; fraud totals were still under investigation and the claim overgeneralizes a community.

claim: The shutdown "cost us two points" of GDP.
explanation: BEA attributed roughly 1 percentage point drag on GDP growth to the shutdown, not 2.

claim: "Cheating is rampant"; noncitizen voting is rampant; SAVE Act needed to stop it.
explanation: FactCheck.org cites DHS/SAVE-style checks showing tiny fractions flagged (and many false positives), contradicting a "rampant" framing.

claim: Tariffs are paid by foreign countries and could replace income taxes.
explanation: NY Fed analysis indicates most tariff costs are passed to U.S. importers/consumers; tariffs are a small revenue share historically and cannot replace income tax at proposed scales.

claim: MFN means Americans will pay the lowest prices in the world for drugs.
explanation: U.S. drug prices are higher than peers, but MFN details are limited and there is no evidence of lowest prices across all drugs for all payers.

claim: Egg prices are down about 60%.
explanation: BLS data show egg prices down about 59% from the March 2025 peak, consistent with a ~60% rounded claim.

claim: Chicken, butter, fruit, hotels, autos, rent are all lower "by a lot" vs when he took office.
explanation: BLS/USDA/FRED indicators show many items are flat or up modestly; "lower by a lot" is not supported broadly.

claim: Drugs coming by sea were "virtually stopped" by a new military campaign.
explanation: No evidence of "virtually stopped"; seizures do not reveal total flow; strikes do not establish drug reduction.

claim: With modest contributions, "Trump Accounts" could exceed $100,000 by age 18.
explanation: Using SEC-style growth assumptions/calculators, typical growth from $1,000 seed money is far below $100k; claim is not realistic as stated.

claim: U.S. "obliterated" / "wiped out" Iran's nuclear program.
explanation: Experts and an initial intel assessment indicated damage but not "obliteration"; the program continues.

claim: He ended "eight wars" in his first 10 months.
explanation: Claim is highly exaggerated; in some cases there were no wars to end and influence varies widely across conflicts.

claim: Tariff revenues are "saving our country" (major fiscal effect).
explanation: Tariffs are not large enough to erase deficits; estimates are large but not deficit-ending and manufacturing job gains did not track with tariffs.
